Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd just... not feel like it. I usually ended up doing things in bursts when I absolutely had to, like when assignments were due. With seven online courses, it felt overwhelming to even think about organizing everything properly, so I kind of just drifted through it.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was pressing. I'd go days without really studying, then suddenly cram when something was due. Regular times never really worked for me because my anxiety would make me avoid sitting down to work, and then I'd feel guilty about it later. Deadlines were basically my only real schedule.
Q3: Usually panic about a deadline or realizing I'd missed something important. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd forgotten about, which honestly made my anxiety worse. Occasionally I'd get an email notification that would remind me I needed to actually look at something. It wasn't really a habit, more like damage control.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a week or two where I tried to make it a daily thing, but it never stuck. My extracurriculars and just life stuff always seemed to take priority, and the LMS started feeling like this thing I was avoiding. The routine never became natural for me.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful because I could watch them while doing other things or lying in bed. I learn better when I can move around or do something with my hands, so just reading materials felt really hard. The videos at least felt like I was absorbing something even when I wasn't fully focused.
Q6: The discussion forums I basically ignored. I only posted three times the whole course because it felt pointless and added to my anxiety about saying something wrong. I also skipped a lot of the extra readings because they felt like too much when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam, it felt worth doing. Anything that seemed optional or vague about its purpose just got pushed aside. I needed to feel like my limited energy was going toward something that would actually count for my grade.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford emotionally. Required ones at least had a deadline pushing me forward, but optional stuff just sat there making me feel bad for not doing it. The required activities had urgency; optional ones just added to my guilt pile.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I downloaded a lot of PDFs and lecture slides to my tablet so I could study without being online. I also used YouTube videos and other websites to understand concepts better because sometimes the course materials didn't click for me. I'd work through things while walking around my room or doing stretches.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over downloaded notes or rewatching videos I'd saved, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts with friends from my extracurriculars who were in similar programs. The LMS wouldn't know I was actually learning during those times.
Q11: All the time. I'd download something once, maybe watch it partially online, then finish it offline multiple times. My internet isn't always reliable, and I preferred having things saved anyway. The LMS probably thought I barely touched those materials when I actually knew them pretty well.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. My anxiety would spike and I'd just shut down, avoiding the LMS entirely because even looking at it stressed me out. I'd feel sad and stuck, knowing I should be doing more but physically unable to make myself do it.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or anxious, the LMS became this thing I couldn't face. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and just managing daily life meant the course often got pushed aside. Confusion about what was actually expected also made me avoid things because I didn't want to do them wrong.
Q14: Many times. I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. My anxiety would have me logged in but not really present, just going through motions to feel like I was doing something. It was engagement on paper but not in my head.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety made starting feel impossible, so I'd wait until the pressure was unbearable. I knew it was self-sabotage but I couldn't seem to break the cycle. Important things felt too heavy to touch until they became emergencies.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd have these bursts of activity right before something was due, which probably looked weird in the logs. Without a deadline, I just couldn't motivate myself to engage with the material at all. It was unhealthy but it was my system.
Q17: Feedback sometimes helped, sometimes made things worse. Good feedback would motivate me briefly, but critical feedback would send my anxiety through the roof and make me avoid that course for days. I started dreading checking for feedback because of how it might affect my mood.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with seven courses running simultaneously. Having so many things to track made me disengage more because I couldn't keep it all straight. A simpler structure with clearer priorities would have helped me stay more connected to the material.
Q19: The flexibility was both good and bad. Good because I could work around my schedule, bad because it let me procrastinate endlessly. Clearer weekly expectations or check-ins might have helped me stay on track. The open structure kind of enabled my drifting tendencies.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my anxious days, logging in repeatedly to check things but being too overwhelmed to actually submit work. You can spend a lot of time in the system without producing anything because you're stuck in a cycle of checking and worrying. Activity doesn't equal productivity when anxiety is involved.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I did okay on exams because I studied offline and in my own way, even though my LMS activity was low. The system doesn't capture the learning that happens outside of it. Someone can be genuinely learning without leaving much of a digital trail.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're online and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually understanding or just scrolling anxiously. They also miss all the offline learning, the conversations with peers, the real thinking that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just completing tasks but wanting to understand the material. For me, real engagement happened in moments when my anxiety wasn't blocking everything, when I could actually be curious about something.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected everything, or how sad I felt about not being the student I wanted to be. It wouldn't show the offline learning or the times I was genuinely interested but couldn't bring myself to log in. It also wouldn't show that despite everything, I was trying in my own messy way.
